dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a diabetic problem at a private residence in [CITY].
Hi ma'am, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My husband has been vomiting and is acting very strange. Low blood sugar, maybe? He's diabetic.
I'm going to ask the patient a few questions to assess his mental status. Sir, can you tell me your name right now?
My...my name is John.
What city do we live in?
[CITY], I think.
Can you show me a peace sign with your right hand?
Good. Your airway is clear and breathing appears unlabored. How long have you been feeling this way?
I'm not exactly sure, but it's been more than a few hours. He was fine until a few hours ago.
Respirations are 20, regular. Equal chest rise noted.
Is it difficult to breathe or feel short of breath at this time?
He's breathing steadily but seems pale and cool to the touch.
Blood pressure is 150 over 70, with a pulse of 73 that's weak and thready.
Skin is cool, clammy, and very diaphoretic. Have you experienced any chest pain or tightening?
Peripheral pulses are present but weak. Let's get some more vitals.
Ma'am, do you mind if we check his blood sugar? I will need a small finger stick sample.
Go ahead.
His blood sugar is 47-depressed, so we need immediate correction.
What is his full name and age?
John Smith. 52 years old. He has been nauseated and vomiting for about two hours. Looks very tired and disoriented.
He has been insulin-dependent type 1 diabetic. Yesterday he skipped an injection because he wasn't feeling right. Hospitalized last week for hypoglycemia.
He's had a malaise and right leg pain for the past three weeks. Vomiting started this morning.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your current pain right now, sir?
Zero. I don't feel any actual pain.
Let's do a 12-lead EKG. First-degree AV block noted in baseline recording.
Ma'am, we're performing a 12-lead EKG. It's non-invasive and safe to assess for cardiac findings.
Noted a visible scar over his sternum. Do you have any rashes or irritation, sir?
Ongoing projectile vomiting. No nasal or ear secretions. Any sensation like a foreign body?
We are going to check blood glucose prior to administering any. This is for safety monitoring.
Pre-administration reading is still at 47. We will start intravenous access and give dextrose.
Blood oxygen saturation at 100%. No need for oxygen supplementation at this time.
Patient is hypoglycemic. Following protocol 3-8-administering D10 and monitoring glucose.
We are establishing IV access in the external jugular to provide rapid reversal of low blood sugar.
Yes, I can help with any securing if needed.
His pulse is now 67 and BP is 132 over 80. Seems more lucid. Are you experiencing any new issues?
Blood sugar is 293 now-watch for rebound hypoglycemia.
First-degree AV block remains stable. Any irregular heart rhythms noticed by the patient?
Agreed. Let's perform a follow-up glucose check in two minutes as safety monitoring.
Sir, where would you like to be transported? [COMPANY_NAME] is an option he requested.
How would you like the patient moved? Options are stretcher or stair chair.
He's on the fifth floor of a home with stairs only.
Understood. We'll move with the stair chair for five flights, with full support at all times.
He's secured with harness for descent-four flights and the outside stairs covered.
That's fine, thank you for being so calm.
ED, this is 52-year-old John Smith, type 1 diabetic with hypoglycemia, vomiting, and occasional disorientation.
He was treated with two D10 IV boluses. Glucose elevated to 293-monitor for reversal.
First-degree AV block documented on EKG. No complications during transport.
He is stable, with all precautions in place for transport.
EKG findings of first-degree AV block consistent. Still no pain reported.
Patient remains stable with vitals trending positive and alert on arrival.
Final reassessment: pulse 56, BP 138/80. Respiration noted at 14, regular.
IV already established and ready in case any changes occur.
No new pain reported and he still rates his discomfort as 0/10.
No incident during transport, patient alert and oriented on arrival, based on Bystander's report.
He has an allergy to Blephamide, though he hasn't been exposed recently.
Regular medications include aspirin, carvedilol, Lipitor, Synthroid, and NovoLog.
When did he last eat or drink before today?
Nothing solid or fluid since yesterday noon.
Respiration noted at 17, regular earlier.
First-degree AV block consistent and stable. No visible dysrhythmia present.
Vital signs remain stable, pulse 56 over 138. Respiration at 14, still regular.
Full protocol 3-8 executed. No complications throughout care.
Patient remains stable, no pain, vitals trending positively with maintenance IV.
Again, no change in pain, he still reports 0/10 discomfort.
Thanks again, your team was very steady and calm through this.
Noted initial combative behavior but now more oriented and cooperative through reassessment.
Just moved him without complication-one more to go.
